agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation policy for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states: placed, invoiced, and shipped.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial agreement to deliver goods or services to a customer, progressing through lifecycle states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing its commitments. Subject to lifecycle constraints."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Once created, it is irreversible per Finance policy."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Per Operations, this is also the moment invoicing occurs."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Represents a point-of-no-return in the PM's cancellation policy."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations practice of generating an invoice at the moment an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from Product defining the cancellation window. A VP could narrow or widen this window.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation should succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible — this is a financial integrity constraint. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would corrupt the financial ledger.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, cancellation must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different stage.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice record must exist immediately after placement completes."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTION: BR-1 says this should succeed (not yet shipped). BR-2 + BR-3 say this must fail (already invoiced at placement). Behavior is undefined until stakeholders resolve the conflict."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails","expected_behavior":"If invoicing at placement fails, does the order exist in an uninvoiced state? If so, BR-1 would allow cancellation but the system is in an inconsistent state per BR-3."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment of a multi-item order","expected_behavior":"Undefined — if some items shipped and some haven't, can the unshipped portion be cancelled? And invoicing status of individual line items is unspecified."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we implement the PM's rule and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, the financial ledger becomes inconsistent. If we implement Finance's rule, customers lose the ability to cancel orders entirely, which may increase chargebacks, refund requests, and customer churn.","operational":"The Operations practice of invoicing at placement creates a zero-width cancellation window when combined with Finance's constraint. This effectively eliminates order cancellation as a feature, contradicting the PM's stated requirement.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If cancellation is impossible from the moment of placement, customer satisfaction and trust will suffer. Support ticket volume will increase.","risk":"Implementing any interpretation without stakeholder alignment risks either financial ledger corruption (if invoiced orders can be cancelled) or customer-facing feature that never actually works (if they cannot). Both outcomes are high-severity.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all if Finance + Ops rules prevail, despite being promised cancellation before shipment."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Ledger integrity at risk if PM's cancellation window is honored for invoiced orders."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing if the contradiction is resolved by decoupling invoice from placement."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of angry customers unable to cancel, or manual reconciliation if invoiced orders are cancelled ad-hoc."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement is undeliverable under current Finance + Ops constraints without a design change."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment. BR-3 ensures every order is invoiced at placement. BR-2 forbids cancellation of invoiced orders. Combined: no order can ever be cancelled, making BR-1 a dead letter. Until stakeholders resolve this, no correct implementation exists. Confidence is below 50 due to the direct contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — it is a logical impossibility, not an edge case","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is a hard financial integrity constraint","The three rules combined produce a zero-width cancellation window"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's rule should yield — this is a business decision, not a technical one","Whether Operations would accept moving invoicing to a later lifecycle stage","Whether Finance would accept a 'credit note' or 'void' mechanism distinct from cancellation"],"assumptions":["All three statements are accurately representing current policy","Invoicing at placement means the invoice exists before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit-note or reversal mechanism that Finance considers distinct from cancellation"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually contradictory. PM says orders can be cancelled before shipment. Operations invoices orders at placement. Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. Therefore: every order is invoiced immediately, and no invoiced order can be cancelled, so no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting the PM's requirement. This is not an edge case; it is a fundamental incompatibility in the stated rules.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Decouple invoicing from placement — invoice at shipment instead, preserving the PM's cancellation window. Operations loses price-lock-at-placement.","B: Introduce a 'void invoice' or 'credit note' mechanism that Finance considers distinct from invoice irreversibility, enabling cancellation with financial reconciliation.","C: Narrow the PM's requirement — cancellation is only possible before invoicing, which under current Ops policy means never. Effectively removes the feature.","D: Introduce a 'pending invoice' state — invoice is reserved at placement but not finalized until shipment, allowing cancellation during the interim."],"recommendation":"Option B or D. Both preserve the PM's cancellation window, respect financial auditability, and avoid forcing Operations to change invoicing timing. Recommend a cross-functional meeting with PM, Finance, and Operations to align on which mechanism is acceptable. Do NOT proceed to implementation until this is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Logical contradiction across three stakeholder requirements creates a zero-width cancellation window","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellation before shipment) is impossible to satisfy when BR-2 (no cancellation of invoiced orders) and BR-3 (invoice at placement) both hold"}]}
